raw_case:
Arthroscopic Excision of a Solitary Intra-articular Osteochondroma Manifesting as a Loose Body: A Presentation of an Extremely Rare Medical Condition
Osteochondroma typically has extra-articular growths at the metaphysis. Intra-articular osteochondroma is extremely uncommon. We report a case of a 55-year-old woman who had been experiencing right knee pain for the past 12 months. An arthroscopy revealed a medial meniscus tear with a loose body in the right knee. It was removed arthroscopically. Histopathology identified it as an osteochondroma. Therefore, intra-articular osteochondroma can be regarded as an uncommon cause of loose bodies in adult patients.

Introduction

Osteochondroma/exostosis is the most common benign bone tumor. It occurs in growing bones and comprises mature bone covered with a cartilaginous cap [1]. It frequently arises from the metaphysis, where it attaches with a stalk and grows toward the diaphysis [2]. It can be solitary or multiple. They are believed to have started as little cartilaginous nodules within the periosteum and are most likely to be developmental abnormalities rather than actual tumors [3]. These lesions are usually asymptomatic and are located near the proximal end of the femur and humerus, positioned extra-articularly [4]. The biological potential of intra-articular osteochondromas affecting the knee joint is poorly understood and frequently misdiagnosed. These lesions, which can be rather large, have histologic traits that point to a malignant process, such as atypia of individual chondrocytes and hypercellularity of the cartilaginous component. Soft tissue tumors frequently develop in close proximity to joints without attaching to the bone. Furthermore, some of these tumors continue to expand even after the skeleton has fully developed [4]. Here, we provide an instance of intra-articular osteochondroma in a 55-year-old female patient complaining of pain and discomfort in the right knee joint, which appeared as a single body during arthroscopy.

Case presentation

A 55-year-old, well-nourished female had pain and discomfort in the right knee. She had a history of falls four years back. Since then, she developed pain in the right knee, which was insidious in onset, gradually progressive, continuous sharp type, and nonradiating. The pain had aggravated in the last year, and the patient was having difficulty climbing stairs. The patient is a known case of hypertension and has been on antihypertensive medications for the last five years. There was no significant family history. On inspection, there was a diffuse swelling over the right knee. The skin over it appeared normal. No scars, discharging sinus, and dilated veins were noted. All the inspection findings were confirmed by palpation. Tenderness was noted over the right medial joint line. Distal pulse and distal movements were present, and the neurovascular deficit was not present. The range of motion of the right and left knee joints was 0 to 120 degrees, and the right knee showed valgus stress.InvestigationsClinical indicators, plain radiography, and MRI were used to diagnose the condition. Anteroposterior and lateral plain radiographs showed an intra-articular loose body (Figures 1A-1C).MRI scan revealed an intra-articular mass (hyperintense to muscle) in the intercondylar notch. The patient underwent arthroscopic meniscus stabilization and removal of loose body, which was sent for histopathological evaluation. Arthroscopy revealed a right knee medial meniscus degenerative tear with a floating loose body about 2 cm × 2 cm in size with no evidence of connectivity with bone (Figure 2).Grossly, it was a globular pale white glistening tissue measuring 2 cm × 2 cm in size. The cut section was hard and showed a central pale-yellow area surrounded by a thin pale-white glistening zone (Figures 3A, 3B).Microscopic examination revealed the presence of mature bony trabeculae enclosing fatty marrow spaces, all of which were encapsulated by a hyaline cartilaginous membrane. There was no evidence of mitotic figures/atypia in the chondrocytes (Figure 4). Thus, the diagnosis of osteochondroma was made.Outcome and follow-upFollowing the arthroscopic removal of the benign tumor, the patient experienced complete relief from her symptoms. Within a month of the surgery, she resumed her routine activities without any subsequent complaints.

Discussion

Osteochondromas are the most common benign bone tumors, typically found near the growth plates of long bones in children and young adults [1]. They are believed to originate from abnormal growth of the epiphyseal growth plate, where a portion of the cartilage becomes displaced and continues to grow independently [2]. On imaging, osteochondromas usually appear as an exostosis with a continuous medullary cavity with the underlying bone. Radiographs often reveal the continuity of the cortex and medullary space between the osteochondroma and the parent bone, which is crucial for diagnosis. MRI is particularly valuable for evaluating the cartilaginous cap and distinguishing osteochondromas from malignant lesions such as chondrosarcoma, especially when symptoms are present or when the cartilaginous cap is thickened [3].Morphologically, osteochondromas vary in size and shape, ranging from a few millimeters to several centimeters. Sessile osteochondromas are broad-based outgrowths, while pedunculated types have a stalk-like base [4]. Histologically, the tumor's cap consists of a cartilaginous layer resembling normal epiphyseal cartilage, which can undergo endochondral ossification. The underlying bone of an osteochondroma is continuous with the cortical and cancellous bone of the host bone, which is a key diagnostic feature [4].Chouliaras et al. [1] described a 32-year-old woman showing a sessile bony growth arising from the superolateral aspect of the distal femur, as revealed by MRI. Arthroscopic excision provided symptomatic relief. Histopathological examination confirmed the diagnosis, showing a benign cartilage cap overlying bone without signs of atypia or malignancy.Morey et al. [2] reported that a 16-year-old patient experienced knee discomfort and clicking. The patient was diagnosed with an intra-articular osteochondroma via arthroscopy. The morphological examination revealed a typical cartilage cap seen in osteochondromas, which was continuous with the underlying bone.Mohanen et al. [4] and Kong et al. [5] discussed rare presentations of intra-articular osteochondromas manifesting as loose bodies within the joints. Patients exhibited joint pain and mechanical symptoms, such as locking or restricted motion. Morphological assessment confirmed the presence of osteochondromas, characterized by a cartilage cap and a bony stalk continuous with the host bone. Histopathology was crucial in differentiating osteochondromas from other conditions, such as synovial chondromatosis or secondary chondrosarcoma.In this case, a 32-year-old female presented with persistent knee pain and mechanical discomfort for three months. MRI revealed a sessile bony growth at the superolateral aspect of the distal femur. This presentation is atypical, as osteochondromas generally affect younger individuals and are usually extra-articular. The symptoms and imaging findings were consistent with previous reports of intra-articular osteochondromas, which tend to cause more pronounced clinical symptoms than their extra-articular counterparts.Accurate diagnosis of intra-articular osteochondromas relies heavily on imaging studies. While plain radiographs can identify extra-articular osteochondromas, they may not provide sufficient details for intra-articular lesions. MRI is crucial to assessing the exact size, location, and impact on surrounding joint structures [5]. Histopathological examination is indispensable in the evaluation of osteochondromas. It provides definitive diagnostic confirmation, differentiates benign tumors from malignant ones, and offers insights into the lesion's potential for malignant transformation [3].The management of symptomatic osteochondromas typically involves surgical excision. Arthroscopic resection is favored for intra-articular osteochondromas due to its minimally invasive nature, reduced postoperative pain, superior cosmetic outcomes, and faster recovery compared to open surgery. Studies by Chouliaras et al. [1] and Kim et al. [3] reported successful outcomes with arthroscopic excision of intra-articular osteochondromas, emphasizing the procedure's efficacy and safety.

Conclusions

Osteochondromas, while common and generally benign, can present with significant clinical symptoms when located intra-articularly. Recognizing the pathological and morphological features of these tumors is crucial for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management. The unique presentation and location of the osteochondroma in our patient underscores the importance of considering this entity in the differential diagnosis of joint-related symptoms and highlights the effectiveness of arthroscopic intervention in such cases.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 55-year-old, well-nourished female.
- **Chief Complaint:** Pain and discomfort in the right knee for the past 12 months.
- **History of Present Illness:** History of pain since a fall four years ago, insidious onset, gradually progressive, sharp, continuous, and nonradiating. Aggravation in the past year, causing difficulty in climbing stairs.
- **Past Medical History:** Hypertension, on antihypertensive medications for five years.
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Diffuse swelling over the right knee with tenderness over the medial joint line. The range of motion was reduced (0–120 degrees). Right knee showed valgus stress, but no neurovascular deficits were noted.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - Plain radiographs: Showed an intra-articular loose body.
  - MRI: Revealed a hyperintense intra-articular mass in the intercondylar notch.
  - Arthroscopy: Found a medial meniscus degenerative tear and a loose body floating (~2 × 2 cm) in the right knee, not connected to any bone.
  - Histopathology: Demonstrated mature bony trabeculae with fatty marrow covered by a hyaline cartilaginous cap. No signs of mitosis or cellular atypia. Diagnosis: Osteochondroma.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Differential Diagnoses:**
   - Meniscus tear (supported by joint pain and valgus stress findings).
   - Loose body due to syndromes like synovial chondromatosis or trauma (apparent on radiographs and MRI).
   - Intra-articular osteochondroma (less common but suggested by imaging findings and clinical presentation).
   - Malignant chondrosarcomas (differentiation essential due to potential malignancy).

2. **Rationale for Considering/Excluding Conditions:**
   - Meniscus Tear: Corroborated by arthroscopy (degenerative medial meniscus tear observed).
   - Synovial Chondromatosis: Excluded due to lack of multiple cartilaginous loose bodies or synovial proliferation.
   - Trauma: Fall history four years ago may have contributed, but arthroscopy found an isolated mass unconnected to trauma.
   - Malignant Lesions (e.g., Chondrosarcoma): Excluded via histopathology showing absence of atypia, mitosis, or thickened cap, consistent with a benign osteochondroma.

3. **Final Steps:**
   - Differential diagnoses narrowed to benign osteochondroma.
   - Arthroscopic removal and histopathology confirmed intra-articular osteochondroma masked as a loose body.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: A solitary intra-articular osteochondroma in the right knee, presenting as a loose body associated with a medial meniscus degenerative tear. Diagnosis based on clinical presentation, imaging (radiographs and MRI), arthroscopic findings, and definitive histopathological analysis.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient's information. She's a 55-year-old female with a history of right knee pain for the past year. The pain started after a fall four years ago and has been getting worse. The pain is sharp and continuous. She's having trouble climbing stairs, which suggests that the knee's function is compromised.

Next, her medical history includes hypertension, but that's managed with medication. No other personal or family history that stands out. So, the focus is on the knee issue.

Physical exam shows swelling and tenderness on the medial joint line. Reduced range of motion (0-120 degrees), and valgus stress. Valgus might indicate some ligamentous laxity or structural deformity, but no neurovascular issues. That makes me think about internal derangements like meniscal tears or ligament injuries.

Looking at the tests: X-ray shows an intra-articular loose body. MRI found a hyperintense mass in the intercondylar notch. Then arthroscopy confirmed a medial meniscus tear and a loose body. The histopathology report says mature bony trabeculae with fatty marrow and a hyaline cartilaginous cap, no signs of malignancy. They called it an osteochondroma.

Wait, osteochondroma is usually an exostosis, a bony outgrowth covered in cartilage. But osteochondromas are typically in the metaphysis of long bones and are connected to the bone. Here, the loose body is intra-articular and not connected to any bone. That's confusing. Maybe it's a synovial osteochondroma or a loose body from osteochondritis dissecans? Or perhaps an intra-articular osteochondroma, which is less common.

The histopathology mentions mature bone and cartilage without atypia, so it's benign. But osteochondromas are usually attached to the bone. However, a loose body could be a detached osteochondroma. Alternatively, maybe it's a synovial chondromatosis, where multiple loose bodies form in the joint. But here there's a single loose body. The meniscal tear could be contributing to the pain and mechanical symptoms.

So, putting it all together: the patient has a degenerative meniscal tear and a loose body. The histopathology confirms that the loose body is an osteochondroma. But given that it's intra-articular and not connected, maybe it's a synovial osteochondroma or a detached osteochondral fragment. However, the histo says osteochondroma. Maybe the original lesion was an osteochondroma that fractured and became a loose body after the fall. Or perhaps it's a primary intra-articular osteochondroma, which is rare but possible. The key here is that the histopathology is definitive for osteochondroma. So despite the unusual presentation, the diagnosis would be osteochondroma as per the histo. However, the main issue here is the loose body and the meniscal tear causing the symptoms. But the question is asking for the final diagnosis based on the provided data. The histopathology result states "Diagnosis: Osteochondroma." So even if it's an atypical presentation, that's the given diagnosis.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient is a 55-year-old female with chronic right knee pain following a fall four years ago, worsening over the past year. Key features include progressive sharp pain, difficulty climbing stairs, and physical exam findings of medial joint line tenderness, reduced range of motion, and valgus stress. This suggests structural joint pathology, such as meniscal injury or degenerative changes.

<step 2> Imaging studies (plain radiographs and MRI) identified an intra-articular loose body and a hyperintense mass in the intercondylar notch. Arthroscopy confirmed a medial meniscus degenerative tear and a detached loose body. These findings align with mechanical joint dysfunction caused by the loose body and meniscal pathology.

<step 3> Histopathology revealed mature bony trabeculae with fatty marrow and a hyaline cartilaginous cap, without cellular atypia. This is characteristic of osteochondroma, a benign osteocartilaginous tumor. While osteochondromas typically arise from bone cortex, the intra-articular location here is unusual but consistent with a detached lesion. The absence of malignancy confirms the benign nature of the mass.

<step 4> The degenerative meniscal tear likely contributed to symptom progression (e.g., pain during stair-climbing). However, the definitive histopathological diagnosis of osteochondroma, combined with imaging and arthroscopic findings, establishes the loose body as the primary pathological entity.

### Answer:
Osteochondroma of the right knee with associated medial meniscus degenerative tear.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient is a 55-year-old female with chronic right knee pain following a fall four years ago, worsening over the past year. Key features include progressive sharp pain, difficulty climbing stairs, and physical exam findings of medial joint line tenderness, reduced range of motion, and valgus stress. This suggests structural joint pathology, such as meniscal injury or degenerative changes.

Imaging studies (plain radiographs and MRI) identified an intra-articular loose body and a hyperintense mass in the intercondylar notch. Arthroscopy confirmed a medial meniscus degenerative tear and a detached loose body. These findings align with mechanical joint dysfunction caused by the loose body and meniscal pathology.

Histopathology revealed mature bony trabeculae with fatty marrow and a hyaline cartilaginous cap, without cellular atypia. This is characteristic of osteochondroma, a benign osteocartilaginous tumor. While osteochondromas typically arise from bone cortex, the intra-articular location here is unusual but consistent with a detached lesion. The absence of malignancy confirms the benign nature of the mass.

The degenerative meniscal tear likely contributed to symptom progression (e.g., pain during stair-climbing). However, the definitive histopathological diagnosis of osteochondroma, combined with imaging and arthroscopic findings, establishes the loose body as the primary pathological entity.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial differential diagnoses include meniscus tear, loose body due to syndromes like synovial chondromatosis or trauma, intra-articular osteochondroma, and malignant chondrosarcomas.
Step 2: Meniscus tear is supported by joint pain and valgus stress findings, corroborated by arthroscopy showing a degenerative medial meniscus tear.
Step 3: Synovial chondromatosis is excluded due to the absence of multiple cartilaginous loose bodies or synovial proliferation.
Step 4: Trauma from a fall four years ago is considered but ruled out as the arthroscopy found an isolated mass not related to trauma.
Step 5: Malignant lesions such as chondrosarcomas are excluded via histopathology, which shows no atypia, mitosis, or thickened caps, consistent with a benign osteochondroma.
Step 6: Differential diagnoses are narrowed to a benign osteochondroma.
Step 7: Arthroscopic removal and histopathology confirm the presence of an intra-articular osteochondroma, initially masked as a loose body.
Step 8: Final diagnosis: A solitary intra-articular osteochondroma in the right knee, presenting as a loose body associated with a medial meniscus degenerative tear.
